metadata.title: Squeeze the position, the momentum spreads (uncertainty is just Fourier)
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Quantum Mechanics Vol. 5 candidate 02. Source: M-06 (The Fourier Transform), Bandwidth Relation and the Uncertainty Principle + Gaussian minimum-uncertainty.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL=narrow/squeezed Gaussian (position); CRIMSON=wide/spread Gaussian (momentum); reciprocal widths show the trade-off
metadata.source: Vol. 5, M-06 (The Fourier Transform), Bandwidth Relation and the Uncertainty Principle + Gaussian minimum-uncertainty
metadata.note: Exclusions: skip Robertson/commutator route entirely; that is a different proof. Only the Fourier bandwidth route: delta-x times delta-k >= 1/2.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B12: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Heisenberg says you cannot know position and momentum at the same time. But this rule was already in mathematics — long before quantum mechanics.

card.dek: Squeeze the position, the momentum spreads

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Heisenberg says you cannot know position and momentum at the same time. But this rule was already in mathematics — long before quantu

beats.narration_text: A Fourier transform predicts that pinching a wave in space must widen it in frequency. Multiply by h-bar and you get Heisenberg. So why is the uncertainty principle called quantum?

card.dek: delta-x times delta-k >= 1/2 (pure math). Multiply by h-bar: delta-x times delta-p >= h-bar/2. Why is this called quantum?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A Fourier transform predicts that pinching a wave in space must widen it in frequency. Multiply by h-bar and you get Heisenberg. So why is the uncertainty princ

beats.narration_text: A wave packet is a sum of pure sine waves. Squeeze the packet into a narrow spike in space and you need many different frequencies to build it.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A wave packet is a sum of pure sine waves. Squeeze the packet into a narrow spike in space and you need many different frequencies to build it.

beats.narration_text: The wider you spread the packet in space, the fewer frequencies it needs. This is not physics — it is the mathematics of wave superposition.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The wider you spread the packet in space, the fewer frequencies it needs. This is not physics — it is the mathematics of wave superposition.

beats.narration_text: The Fourier transform pairs position and wavenumber. A narrow Gaussian in x transforms to a wide Gaussian in k. They breathe in reciprocal proportion.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The Fourier transform pairs position and wavenumber. A narrow Gaussian in x transforms to a wide Gaussian in k. They breathe in reciprocal proportion.

beats.narration_text: The bandwidth bound: delta-x times delta-k is at least one half. The Gaussian saturates this bound exactly — it is the most tightly packed wave shape possible.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The bandwidth bound: delta-x times delta-k is at least one half. The Gaussian saturates this bound exactly — it is the most tightly packed wave shape possible.

beats.narration_text: In quantum mechanics, momentum is h-bar times wavenumber. That single substitution converts the math theorem into Heisenberg's inequality — no new physics required.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: In quantum mechanics, momentum is h-bar times wavenumber. That single substitution converts the math theorem into Heisenberg's inequality — no new physics requi

beats.narration_text: The uncertainty principle applies to sound, light, and radar — any wave system. Quantum mechanics inherits it by identifying momentum with wavenumber.

props.headline: The uncertainty principle applies to sound, light, and radar — any…

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The uncertainty principle applies to sound, light, and radar — any wave system. Quantum mechanics inherits it by identifying momentum with wavenumber.

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: a Gaussian packet with position width 0.5 nanometers. The Fourier bound forces a momentum spread of at least h-bar over one nanometer. Halve the width — the momentum spread doubles.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Illustrative: a Gaussian packet with position width 0.5 nanometers. The Fourier bound forces a momentum spread of at least h-bar over one nanometer. Halve the w

beats.narration_text: Squeezing a wave in space widens it in frequency — that is the Fourier bandwidth bound. Multiply by h-bar and you have Heisenberg. The uncertainty is Fourier, not mystery.

card.dek: Fourier bandwidth: delta-x times delta-k >= 1/2. Multiply by h-bar: uncertainty principle.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Squeezing a wave in space widens it in frequency — that is the Fourier bandwidth bound. Multiply by h-bar and you have Heisenberg. The uncertainty is Fourier, n
